cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: A legislature composed of two chambers or houses

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The lower chamber of Congress with 435 members, apportioned by state population, who serve two-year terms

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The upper chamber of Congress with 100 members, two per state, who serve six-year terms

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The presiding officer of the House of Representatives who controls the legislative agenda and is second in presidential succession

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The senator who presides over the Senate in the absence of the Vice President; largely a ceremonial role

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The party leader in each chamber who sets the legislative agenda and coordinates party strategy

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: A party official in Congress who counts votes, pressures members to support party positions, and ensures attendance

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: A permanent congressional committee that specializes in a particular policy area, such as Armed Services or Judiciary

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: A temporary committee created to investigate a specific issue or event

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: A committee formed with members from both the House and Senate to resolve differences between competing versions of a bill

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: A Senate tactic in which a senator speaks for an extended time to delay or prevent a vote on a bill

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: A Senate procedure requiring 60 votes to end debate and bring a bill to a final vote, overcoming a filibuster

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The practice of legislators trading votes on different bills to secure mutual support for their respective priorities

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: Government spending on projects that benefit a specific congressional district, often used to gain political support

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: An unrelated amendment attached to a bill, often to force passage or defeat of the provision

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The redrawing of congressional district boundaries, typically after each census, to reflect population changes

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The manipulation of district boundaries to benefit a particular political party or group

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The redistribution of House seats among the states based on the census, conducted every ten years

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The formal process by which the House of Representatives charges a federal official with misconduct

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The Senate's constitutional role in confirming presidential appointments and ratifying treaties

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The president's formal power to reject a bill passed by Congress, sending it back unsigned

cards.prompt: An indirect veto that occurs when the president takes no action on a bill within 10 days while Congress has adjourned

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The constitutional provision allowing Congress to pass a vetoed bill with a two-thirds vote in both chambers

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: A presidential directive that manages operations of the federal government and has the force of law without congressional approval

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: A written statement issued by the president when signing a bill into law, offering the president's interpretation of the legislation

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: A pact between the president and a foreign head of state that does not require Senate ratification, unlike a treaty

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The claimed right of the president to withhold information from Congress or the courts to protect national security or executive deliberations

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The 1973 law requiring the president to notify Congress within 48 hours of deploying troops and limiting deployment to 60 days without congressional authorization

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The president's power to grant clemency to individuals convicted of federal crimes

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The president's use of public visibility and media access to rally public opinion and pressure Congress to act

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The annual address in which the president reports on the condition of the nation and outlines the legislative agenda before a joint session of Congress

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The power of courts to determine whether governmental actions are constitutional, established in Marbury v. Madison (1803)

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The authority of a court to hear a case for the first time, as opposed to hearing an appeal

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The authority of a court to review decisions made by lower courts

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: A formal order by the Supreme Court directing a lower court to send up the records of a case for review, requiring at least four justices to agree

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The opinion of the court that represents the view of the majority of justices and establishes binding precedent

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: An opinion by a justice who agrees with the majority's conclusion but for different reasons

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: An opinion by a justice who disagrees with the majority's decision and reasoning

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The legal principle meaning 'let the decision stand,' where courts follow established rulings from prior cases

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: A judicial philosophy in which judges interpret the Constitution broadly, considering contemporary circumstances and social needs

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: A judicial philosophy in which judges defer to the elected branches and interpret the Constitution based on the framers' original intent

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The constitutional provision granting federal judges appointment for life during 'good behavior,' insulating them from political pressure

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The informal tradition in which a senator from the president's party can block a judicial nomination in their home state

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The complex system of federal agencies, offices, and departments that implement and administer government policy

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: A federal agency created by Congress to regulate a specific sector of the economy, operating with relative independence from the president

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: A government agency that operates like a business, providing services to the public and charging fees, such as the U.S. Postal Service

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The system of hiring federal employees based on qualifications and competitive exams rather than political connections, established by the Pendleton Civil Service Act

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: A mutually beneficial relationship among a congressional committee, an interest group, and a bureaucratic agency that shapes policy in a specific area

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: A fluid, open network of activists, policy experts, interest groups, and government officials who influence policy in a specific area, less rigid than an iron triangle

cards.prompt: Congress's monitoring of the executive branch and federal agencies to ensure they are implementing laws as intended

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The legal authorization for a federal agency or program to exist and operate

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The process by which Congress allocates specific amounts of money to authorized programs and agencies

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The power of bureaucratic agencies to fill in the details of broadly written legislation through specific rules and regulations

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: Marbury v. Madison (1803)
cards.answer: Landmark Supreme Court case that established judicial review, giving the Court power to declare laws unconstitutional

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: An example of the president using informal power to influence Congress
cards.answer: Using the bully pulpit to rally public support for legislation

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: An example of Congress checking the power of the president
cards.answer: Senate refusing to ratify a treaty

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: An example of the judiciary checking the power of Congress
cards.answer: Supreme Court striking down a law as unconstitutional through judicial review

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The exclusive power of the House of Representatives to introduce bills that raise revenue

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The House committee that sets the terms and conditions under which a bill will be debated on the floor

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: An example of a check on the judicial branch
alternate_answers: Constitutional amendment overturning a court ruling
Congress changing the number of Supreme Court justices

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The practice of allowing senators from the president's party to effectively veto judicial nominees from their home state

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: A model of representation in which legislators act as trustees, using their own judgment on issues rather than strictly following voter preferences

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: A model of representation in which legislators strictly follow the wishes and instructions of their constituents

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: A model of representation in which legislators act as both trustees and delegates depending on the issue

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: A treaty ratified by two-thirds of the Senate that is binding under international law, unlike an executive agreement

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The informal Rule of Four requiring at least four Supreme Court justices to agree before the Court will hear a case

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: A legal brief filed by a third party not directly involved in a case, offering additional perspectives or information to the court

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The president's role as the chief architect of foreign policy and commander of the armed forces

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: An example of the expansion of presidential power beyond the Constitution's explicit grants
cards.answer: Issuing executive orders to direct federal agencies without legislation
alternate_answers: Executive agreements bypassing Senate treaty ratification

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: Congressional committees with members from both chambers that coordinate between the House and Senate on specific topics

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The president's constitutional duty to ensure that federal laws are properly executed and enforced

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: An example of bureaucratic accountability to Congress
cards.answer: Congressional hearings investigating an agency's performance
alternate_answers: GAO audit of a federal agency

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: A voting arrangement in Congress between two or more members in which they agree to support each other's bills

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The fifteen executive departments headed by secretaries who advise the president and are confirmed by the Senate

cards.prompt: A legal document submitted by the U.S. Solicitor General representing the government's position before the Supreme Court
